speaker: interviewee
text: So in 1944-- of June 1944, the Russians marched back into Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- the-- the people-- the family announced that, you know, the Russians were there and that you're free now. So I walked out, and again, approaching it as an adventure, and I went and I started going into the streets and, you know, meeting people and asking. And they-- you know, each-- many of the-- the ones who were hidden went out of hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Set the stage. How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I am 14-- 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're just let out the door?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm just let out the door. Well, I-- you know, they-- they treated me nicely, these-- these people, and so they-- they-- in fact I think one of the daughters accompanied me. And we started meeting people. There was a Jewish community that was set up right away. And it was a small city, so we really knew right away, you know, where the gathering place was. And when I went out there, I-- I met my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this gathering place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Kaunas. And it was, I believe, on [INAUDIBLE]. And so the Jewish people started coming in. I-- this happened a few days. I was over there, I went over there and went back. And I-- and I met my Uncle Leiba, who was the doctor, my mother's-- my mother's brother, and his wife, who was a dentist, Hanna Taft, and their little boy, [? Mathis-- ?] no, it wasn't [? Mathis. ?] It was Yasha, I believe. And then two my-- two of my cousins, who were also their nieces and nephews, who also were in hiding in all different places. And so we all survived, and they all gathered. And my uncle was very well-known as a doctor in [INAUDIBLE], in that area, and he was very liked. I would say that, you see, the-- the Lithuanians were nice to us because my mother really never had an enemy. She was always, you know, she was very kind and very good-natured and always believed that-- that you never fight with anyone, you never argue because you have two losers. If you make peace, you have two winners, and that was her philosophy in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was never any-- any harm done to us or any animosity towards my family. And to this day when I went back, they-- everyone said, well, who doesn't know the Ceitlin and then the Taft family, you know, with admiration? So my uncle set up practice right away. He got an apartment since he was a doctor. She was a dentist. And they started getting-- the Lithuanians were just flocking in to them. You know. They took you in? You stayed with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and my-- my-- my aunt and uncle said this family wanted to keep me and they said, stay with us. We'll send you to school. We'll do-- you know, we'll educate you. And my uncle said, no, whatever's left of us, we have to stay together. So I left a week later or so and I went to live with my aunt and uncle Taft. And I, again, education, since it was foremost in our home, I immediately enrolled in-- in a school, in a gymnasium. And I skipped all the years that I did not go to school because I insisted that I'm not going to go back in where the children are. I'm going to go-- I mean, where I-- where I belong from an educational level. I-- I wanted to be in my chronological age group. And I did. And I learned Lithuanian, because everything was Lithuanian. I was the only Jewish girl in our [INAUDIBLE] gymnasium. And I worked day and night, studied day and night, and my aunt and uncle were, again, very nice to me, provided me with all. You know, we-- we had a little apartment. We all lived-- let's see, one, two, three, four, I think there were six of us in one room. And one room was a kitchen, one was a living room, and one was an-- an office for my uncle and one was an office for my aunt. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn of your parents and your-- the rest of your family? Did you learn anything about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew-- of course we didn't-- in those days we had no correspondence, so I didn't know anything about my parents. Someone mentioned that they were-- they were sent out to Siberia, but we had no correspondence at all. And-- and we didn't really-- and the war was still on. The concentration camps were still on. It was 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew that my Aunt Paula, Chassia, Osher, they were all sent out. Where, we didn't know anything about them. So we stuck together. My aunt-- Leiba and Hanna and the children and-- my-- my cousins. And I went to school, and it was of utmost importance to me to study and to, you know, do well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would grab hold of everybody I-- I could find to help me with math, with history, with-- to catch up and everything that I needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Russians running the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Lithuanians were running the school. Russian was a language that was required, but Lithuanian-- it was a Lithuanian school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there much of a Russian presence? Did you see--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all over. There was [INAUDIBLE]. There was secret police, and you had to be very careful what you said, how you said. And you couldn't earn very much money or else they would be confiscated. My aunt and uncle did well financially, I mean, according to their-- to the standards. Because he was a-- you know, he immediately got fees for his medical practice and my aunt for dental.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I remember that you couldn't even buy anything because in fear the Russians would come and they would-- they would or send you to prison. Because they immediately started delving as to where did you get the money? What did you do? We couldn't talk out loud about anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw everything, but I-- I couldn't-- you know, I knew how to-- how to keep quiet. And God forbid that anybody would find out that my parents were sent to Siberia, because then I would be a prisoner as well. Well, then this went on for a year, and conditions were very bad all around us. Again, I was pretty lucky-- I sometimes say that I was born with a lucky star above me-- that no matter under the circumstances I still, you know, had it relatively good.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had enough to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had enough to eat. We had some clothing. You know, now-- of course, one dress, you know, a pair of shoes, maybe some-- something to change into something else. A lot of leftovers from-- you know, from one because my cousin also lived with us and she was two years younger than I was. So-- however, I don't remember ever really bemoaning the fact that I-- because we were all in the same situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing that I remember really of-- I was very sick at home. When I lived at home, I had every disease you can think of. But nothing that-- that was really outstanding that I-- I can think of while I was with my aunt and uncle-- a cold here and there, but not much otherwise. But I remember many fillings in my teeth. My aunt would always fill it for me. Well, then the rumors went around that the Aliyah Bet was quite active in Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us what that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Aliyah Bet was an organization that was formed to get the remaining Eastern European Jews out of Lithuania into Israel-- into Palestine in those days. That was 1945. And one of my aunts', Hanna, the one who was a dentist, best friend was somebody whose name was Schmuel. I don't remember his last name. And he was in charge of the Aliyah Bet. So he was really the one who was organizer-- who was organizing groups of people to go out, underground movement-- it was an underground movement-- to form groups of Jewish people, to get the-- smuggle them out from Lithuania into Poland, where Poland was freer. This was already after the Germans abdicated, so Poland was also under was under Russian, but he was much more liberal. The Poles never somehow gave in as much as the Lithuanians, and so when someone escaped into Poland, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were already-- it was easier for them to leave Poland to go out. The-- the way of escape was from Lithuania to Poland to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before VE Day. I'm a little confused.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was after VE Day. I was-- when I left Kaunas was in December of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember VE Day before we move ahead? Do you remember when you knew the-- the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can remember-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it wasn't-- I can't-- I, myself, cannot remember that distinctly. I remember hearing about it from other people, reading about it, because we were liberated, you see, in 1944. So we were-- there wasn't that much of a change, because it was-- the Russians were there and the Russians were there again. So as far as I was concerned, it wasn't too much of a change for me. I remember hearing that, you know, the concentration camps were open. We didn't really have too many-- too many-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't know too much about it, because everything was very secretive. There was no press-- it was forbidden to write anything about it-- or radios or anything like it. So it was rumors what we heard from people here and there. And it was-- everything was in an uproar in those days, in 1944, '45. You know, between 1940-- June-- between the times that the Russians came in in 1944 and liberated us-- we were-- we-- we were-- we felt that the Russians liberated us-- to the time that, you know, the end of the war, to V Day, so there wasn't that much of a transition in our lives right in Lithuania. And people really didn't know too much about it. We heard rumors here and there. There were underground letters that came. And that's how the Aliyah Bet was formed. Now Schmuel was very active in that movement, and he was a very close friend of my aunt's, and in fact he was a professor and he would come and he would really help me with my studies as well, so I was very close to him. And he was-- I would say-- I was 14-- 14. And he was-- must have been in like those days about 28, 25, something like that. And again, I looked up to him as a man, you know, for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a romance with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- perhaps then a little bit maybe, but he was always very kind to me. You know, he would help me with everything. And so he wanted to take my whole family out, my aunt and uncle, underground. And in those days we used to call them transport. There were many transports that left Lithuania. And transports consisted of a group of about 30 people who usually went on a pickup truck, on a large truck, and-- covered truck, and they would go for about, I would say, an hour or two ride to-- to the border, to the woods. And then we would have to walk through the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now are you describing your experience now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's how it was. I mean that's what-- how you-- how you went underground. But that's how I-- my experience, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you decide to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So that's why I wanted to say how it consisted-- what it involved. Well, many transports-- I wouldn't say many, but a number of transports were caught because you had to walk through the border, through the woods. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were caught by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they immediately called in the upper-- the-- the higher officials, and they were taken into prisons and beaten and sent back into-- into prison camps, Russian labor camps. And incidentally, my father-- well, while I was-- when I was liberated I found out later on that-- well, I didn't have-- I didn't know too much about my parents, but I suppose later on-- no, that-- in thinking back, it was really after in Germany later on I found out how my father died in a Russian concentration-- in a Russian labor camp. But anyhow, let's proceed with the story. So my aunt-- so Schmuel was-- so many-- some transports were caught and, you know, go-- got-- having to go back into labor camps and-- and prisons and so on. My aunt and uncle established themselves quite well in Kaunas, so Schmuel wanted to take them out, and my aunt felt that she really didn't want to go through this again.

speaker: interviewer
text: After all, they were already situated and things weren't so bad, and they had little children, so they wanted to stay. And Schmuel says, you know what? Why don't you let Marcia go? She said, well, Marcia is always very independent-- ask her. Why don't you ask her? I said, yes, sure. It was an adventure. You know. I-- I'll go. So I remember that, you know, my aunt Hanna, I-- I couldn't take very much clothing with me, but she sewed some we used to call them [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. She had the money, whatever you had since you couldn't have very much money, was converted into buying gold coins, and she sewed some gold coins into my clothing. In case something should happen and I need some money, you know, I could always convert it into money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I kissed her goodbye, kissed the whole family goodbye, and went in this transport. And Schmuel was there. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Schmuel's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't remember. I met him later on in Israel, and I think his name was Golden, [? Golub, ?] something like that. It started with a G. I do have it put away, and I should really look at it. Anyhow, so we went with this-- with this truck to the woods, and while in the woods we start-- we had to start walking. And there were some small children. I didn't know any of the people outside of Schmuel. And there were little babies, and some of the babies started to cry, and the-- and the Russians caught us. They heard the crying and they immediately came with their guns extended and they caught us. It was like 2 o'clock in the morning and they rounded us up, and I, not having anybody there, decided, well-- again, my mind was very active-- I was going to throw myself into the bushes and escape. You know. I thought, well, when the people were all rounded up and taken to the headquarters that I was going to, you know, smuggle myself through. Well unfortunately, one of the women-- our Jewish women and the transport-- said there was a child over there, so the Russians came looking through the bushes and found me and started beating me with his-- with his big pistol. And I thought that was already the end of me, and he thought so, too. So he just left me in the bushes. Because I was-- I had-- I did have a knapsack on me, and he was beating me on the knapsack. So that kind of-- I escaped the heavy blows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you wounded?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was wounded. I had-- you know, I was bleeding. I had-- I had swellings. But I wasn't-- you know, I wasn't-- nothing was broken. I wasn't incapacitated. So, you know, when the Russian walked away, I just got myself-- picked myself up and threw myself back into the transport, you know, with the people. And Schmuel was there and he kind of looked out for me. And we went to those headquarters and he says, no, we're-- we're not going to let you go. And we were very prepared for them, because every transport had [NON-ENGLISH] and big watches that ticked-- I remember that-- and that's what the Russians liked. You know, they-- they were very simple people-- the-- the policemen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Schmuel and some other big [INAUDIBLE] there, and they-- we-- we tried to, you know, bribe them with this, with that, and telling them all you were not going to gain anything by-- by sending us off into prisons. Why don't-- we will give you all this and let us go? Which they finally did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so again, my lucky star was above me. And we went through to Poland. From--

speaker: interviewer
text: Back in the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was another-- no, no, the truck left. That truck that took us to the woods, he-- he-- he turned around and went back to court Kaunas. See? So we-- we smuggled ourselves. We had to walk through the woods. And the borders were very-- very simple. I mean, you know, we-- there was no gate, or maybe there was a fence in some parts. So-- however, where the headquarters of-- for the Russian police was, that was-- that was Lithuanian on one side and the other side was Poland, on the other side of the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we trespassed the wood-- the woods and when they let us go, they said, all right, go. And then we took a train from there into Warsaw. And then from Warsaw, we took the train into Lodz. And there I-- we had to stay there until we were going into Munich. And you mention about if I was ever sick. Well, there I came down with the measles. With chicken pox. Chicken pox? Let me see. No, measles. Measles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I remember that in those days, you felt that you couldn't-- when you had measles everything had to be in a dark situation, in a dark, closed environment. So there were drapes and there were-- there were shutters. And Schmuel stayed with me. And then those-- and in that-- during that time we did have a little romance. I was 15. No, 14. 15, 15. Right. And then he stayed with me for three days-- everybody went on-- until most of the measles were gone. And then we went to Munich. And we came to Munich I found my Uncle Osher. He-- [INAUDIBLE]. He survived Dachau, and he was there with my cousin Wolf Prensky.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find them?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Munich, we started to inquire. And found-- and I met some people already who were from Lithuania there. There was a-- there was a DP camp there in Munich, and they said, oh, you know, your Uncle Osher is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay in the DP the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- I think, perhaps, one day, because my Uncle Osher also knew somehow that I was coming. So he came, picked me up, and, of course, took me to [? Batels. ?] It's a suburb of-- it's-- well, it's a beautiful town in Bavaria, which is about an hour from Munich. And so he took me to Bavaria, and I remember that I thought this was a palace. My uncle, again, like in the ghetto, he was always knew how to organize himself, how to do public relations. He was actually distributor-- a manufacturer's distributor before the war, so he had many accounts in-- in-- overseas. So he immediately started, when he survived-- when he was liberated, he immediately started writing letters. He has a memory like never ends. To this day, he's alive. He lives in Vancouver. And in fact, I'm going to go over there and make a tape of-- of my family, of things-- because he remembers everything. And-- and as I said, I-- even though sometimes things come to me when I concentrate hard, but one of my ways of survival is to block things out. So I purposely didn't want to remember things. And just said, OK, it's a new life. You go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here you are in Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I am in Munich in [? Batels ?] and, yeah. So we come into this house and he had a beautiful house. And they had a room already.

speaker: interviewer
text: He already a beautiful house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, a beautiful house.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're in 1940--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. Well, it was-- it was January 1940-- it was December. I left Lithuania, so it was either the end of December or beginning of January of 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: So six months after the end of the war, only a few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right. December-- January of 1946, right. So shortly after he was liberated, right, he was now in the concentration camp, from what I understand. He was also a kapo, which is a Jewish policeman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What camp was he in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Dachau. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell about you now. Here you are. You're in this house.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So here I am in this house, and I remember walking in there, as I said, and I thought it was a palace. My uncle had a room for me, and-- beautifully appointed, and says, now you can decorate it whichever way you want to. And he had a lady friend that he-- that he met in Dachau who was working in the kitchen. And she was the one who really helped him, because my cousin Wolf was with him, and he contracted meningitis in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in order to get some medication and some food, she would help them out. So she was with him. They were liberated together. And so Lotte was her name. And his wife and son died in Stutthof, in the children's-- you know, in the children's section, and the children-- with the children. She didn't want to be separated from the child, so the Germans said, go, both of you. So he was-- he knew that his wife wasn't there, so he married this lady, Lotte, and my-- my cousin was there and I was there. And I remember Lotte was very nice to me, and she immediately called in a seamstress and-- and, you know, and had some clothes made for me. And I felt I had three dresses or two dresses and shoes and I felt like I was really in a palace, you know, my own room, and I remember decorating it and wanting to be beautiful. So you weren't going to go to Palestine? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was on my way to Palestine, but we had to wait. You know, this was January of 1946. And the-- there were-- the-- the-- the ship was going to go the-- what ship was it? The I was supposed to be on the ship, and so my cousin and I, we would go into Munich and meet the Jewish community, you know, there. And incidentally, again I said, no, I have to go to school. So I enrolled, and I-- I went to look for a German university, and-- and again, said I finished gymnasium, which I really didn't. But I just wanted to-- I knew that the only way I could get ahead was through education, and so I said, I have to arm myself with the best education I possibly can. And so anyhow, I-- I was on my way and waiting to go on that ship when my aunt got word and my aunt-- my uncle was also corresponding with my aunt in New York, my Aunt Assia, Assia Goder. And she heard that I had come to Germany, to Munich, so she immediately went-- there were no doors locked for her-- And she was actually a very reserved person. But when she heard that, you know, the remains of her family, she didn't have any children and she immediately got papers and a visa to come to Germany. And she came to Germany, to Munich, to [? Batels ?] and had a big fight with my uncle because my uncle wanted us to go to Palestine. But she said, no, they're coming to the United States. So she immediately made out papers, went to see the Jewish organization, and through the-- through HIAS, we were on the list to come to the-- as foster children-- we were 15-- to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to wait a couple of months. And then we went to-- we went to the pier-- the pier in Bremen, and from Bremen, my cousin Wolf and I, came with the ship with Bremen, I believe it was, what was name of the ship-- to the United States.
